coredata.dc:title: Preparation of pH-responsive fluorescent carbon dots under extreme alkaline conditions
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Dalu
Ni, Yeyan
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) were prepared via a one-step solvothermal method using L-aspartate as the carbon source and ammonia as the solvent. The prepared CDs exhibited pH-sensitive properties and a color changing response to extreme alkaline pH ranges (12.0–14.0). O-related defect and N-related defect states induced by the protolated amino groups on the CD surfaces alternately dominated the fluorescence emission with a change in pH value, and the luminescence of different defect states was greatly affected by the excitation wavelength, which endowed the CDs with different pH-dependent fluorescence emission behavior under different light excitation. Based on the fluorescent emission behavior, which could be adjusted with a change in pH, the CDs showed potential application value as an instant inspection.

Luminescence
Carbon dots
pH-responsive
Extreme alkaline conditions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphological and structural analysis of the CDs
pH-dependent luminescence performance analysis of the CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CDs. (b) The particle size distribution of CDs. (c) XRD pattern of CDs.
(a) Photographs showing fluorescence images under UV light and (b-p) fluorescent scan of CDs obtained at different pH from 1 to 14.
(a) Photographs showing fluorescence images under UV light and (b-m) fluorescent scan of CDs obtained at different pH from 12 to 14.

(a) Dependence of PL intensity on pH (change from 12 to 14) under excitation at 360 and 440 nm. Emission spectra of 1-CDs solution at different pH values ranging from 12 to 14 under (b) 340 nm excitation and (c) 440 nm excitation.

head.ce:title: Preparation of pH-responsive fluorescent carbon dots under extreme alkaline conditions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Suzhou Institute of Trade& Commerce, Suzhou 215009, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Suzhou Institute of Trade& Commerce, Suzhou 215009, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) were prepared via a one-step solvothermal method using L-aspartate as the carbon source and ammonia as the solvent. The prepared CDs exhibited pH-sensitive properties and a color changing response to extreme alkaline pH ranges (12.0–14.0). O-related defect and N-related defect states induced by the protolated amino groups on the CD surfaces alternately dominated the fluorescence emission with a change in pH value, and the luminescence of different defect states was greatly affected by the excitation wavelength, which endowed the CDs with different pH-dependent fluorescence emission behavior under different light excitation. Based on the fluorescent emission behavior, which could be adjusted with a change in pH, the CDs showed potential application value as an instant inspection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of carbon nanomaterial, have attracted significant attention due to their specific advantages such as wide source of raw materials, low cost, excellent physical and chemical properties [1–3]. pH-dependent photoluminescence properties are considered as their most interesting properties. The photoluminescence intensity or spectrum of CDs would vary with changes in pH value [4,5]. pH plays a key role in industry, agriculture, environment, and biomedicine [6]. pH-sensitive fluorescent probes reported have been mostly based on the change in pH value in the system, to enhance or reduce fluorescence intensity. This change must be achieved by detection using a fluorescence spectrophotometer accurately. Visual pH probes are nondestructive, with in situ, sensitive, and visually observable characteristics. Subsequently, many visual pH probes have been detected that can respond in a mild pH range of 4–11 [7,8]. However, few probes can be applied under extreme pH conditions, possibly due to the inability of sensor materials to maintain photochemical stability in strongly acidic and alkaline environments.

In addition, when preparing pH-sensitive CDs, small-molecule compounds containing amino groups are typically used as carbon sources or dopants [9]. These small-molecule compounds, such as o-phenylenediamine and ethylenediamine, are often highly biotoxic and require equally toxic organic solvents for dissolution. This preparation process places considerable pressure on the environment. Herein, L-aspartate selected as a green and low-cost carbon and nitrogen source, and synthesized extreme alkaline pH-responsive CDs through the solvothermal method in the presence of ammonia, providing a new approach for the effective utilization of biomass resources.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural analysis of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were synthesized using L-aspartate as the raw material. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the synthesized CDs had a quasi-circular shape, good dispersion, and uniform size. The HRTEM image in the illustration demonstrated a lattice spacing of 0.33 nm, corresponding to the (002) crystal face of graphite [6]. As shown in Fig. 1b, from the 100 individual particles selected at random, the size of the CDs was mainly in the range of 5.8–12.6 nm, indicating that the CDs had a wide particle size distribution range. As shown in Fig. 1c, the XRD pattern of the CDs exhibited a peak envelope at about 26°, which corresponded to the (002) face of the graphite lattice structure [10]. The lithium-like ink crystal structure formed in the CDs possibly had an excellent relationship with the synthesis conditions. The stretching vibrations of C–C (1617 cm−1), C–N (1403 cm−1), and C=N/C=O (1718 cm−1) could also be observed from the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S1), indicating that CDs undergo dehydration and condensation reactions during carbonization [10], resulting in the formation of highly graphitized structures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH-dependent luminescence performance analysis of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: By regulating the pH (pH 1–14), the prepared CD solution exhibited a unique PH-dependent luminescence phenomenon. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the CD solution exhibited excellent and stable blue fluorescence emission in a wide pH range (1–12). Under strong alkaline conditions (pH 12–14), significant fluorescence color changes were observed with an increase in pH value. The CD solution changed from blue to green, and finally, the fluorescence was nearly quenched. To verify the discoloration observed by the naked eye, the fluorescence emission profiles of the CDs in different pH ranges were recorded by a fluorescence spectrophotometer for comparison. In the pH range of 1–12, the optimal excitation and emission positions of the CDs did not change. When the excitation wavelength was 352 nm, the strongest emission spectrum was obtained, which was located at 448 nm. However, when the pH value was increased to 13, the fluorescence emission spectrum of the CDs changed. The optimal emission peak red-shifted to 503 nm, and a small peak appeared at 413 nm, corresponding to the optimal excitation wavelength of 383 nm. The changes in excitation and emission of the CDs in different pH intervals indicated that different defect states dominated the luminescence behavior of the CDs, depending on the change in environmental pH. Combined with the UV–Vis absorption and emission spectra of the CDs (Fig. S2), the fluorescence emission behavior of the CDs was possibly related to the surface defect state induced by C = O/C = N.

To further study the pH-dependent behavior of the CDs under extreme alkaline conditions, we adjusted the pH value of the CDs to 12.0–14.0, with a pH interval of 0.2. As shown in Fig. 3 a, with a gradual increase in pH value, significant color changes were observed with the naked eye, where the color gradually changed from blue to green, and then gradually underwent quenching. To verify the discoloration observed by the naked eye, fluorescence emission profiles of CDs in a narrow pH range were recorded by a fluorescence spectrophotometer for comparison. As shown in Fig. 3b, when pH = 12 and the excitation wavelength is 340 nm, the optimal emission wavelength at 448 nm reaches its maximum value. As the pH gradually increases, the original emission peak at 448 nm was divided into two main peaks: one undergoes a redshift from 448 to 503 nm with a gradual decrease in fluorescence, while the other is fixed at 413 nm with little change in peak intensity and position. When the pH value reaches 14 and the excitation wavelength is 400 nm, the optimal emission wavelength at 503 nm reaches its maximum value. This interesting phenomenon indicated that two emission sites were present in the CDs, which further confirmed that the O-related defect and N-related defect states in the CDs alternately induced fluorescence emission behavior.

To further verify the fluorescence emission behavior in extreme alkaline conditions, the fluorescence emission changes of CDs under short (340 nm) and long (400 nm) wavelength excitation were recorded. In Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence intensity obtained at 340 nm initially increased with the increase of pH from 12.0 to 12.8, and then the further increase of pH caused a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity. However, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreases significantly with the increase of pH value at 400 nm. Overall, the correlation between the fluorescence intensity obtained under different excitation and pH value is different, indicating that CDs have different pH-dependent fluorescence emission behavior under short wavelength excitation and long wavelength excitation. To further illustrate the effect of excitation wavelength on pH-dependent emission properties, the emission spectra of CDs in different pH solutions were recorded at 340 nm and 400 nm respectively. In Fig. 4b, the emission spectra obtained at 340 nm excitation of CDs show that its fluorescence emission changes significantly with the increase of pH. As pH increases from 12.0 to 12.8, the corresponding emission peak is divided into two peaks from 448 nm, which are located at 503 nm and 413 nm respectively. With the increase of pH value, the peak strength at 503 nm could be enhanced first and then decreased, but the peak strength at 413 nm did not change. In contrast, the peak position of the optimal emission peak at 400 nm excitation is almost unaffected by changes in pH (Fig. 4c). According to the proposed fluorescence emission mechanism, it is believed that the different defect state luminescence depends on the excitation wavelength, which is the main reason for the different pH-dependent fluorescence emission behavior of CDs.

The fluorescence emission behavior induced by alternating O-dependent defect states and N-dependent defect states can be further explained by absorption spectra. As shown in Fig. S3, the absorption spectra obtained by CDs in the pH range of 1–11 show absorption bands in the range of 275–300 nm and 325–350 nm. When the pH value reaches 12.0, the absorption band at 275–300 nm weakens and disappears at 325–350 nm. With the increase of pH value, the absorption band at 275–300 nm was weakened. This change in absorption spectra suggests that an increase in pH deactivates one of the defect states, further proving that pH-dependent luminescence behavior is related to the transition of the defect states.

In summary, CDs based on L-aspartate were prepared through a one-step solvothermal method. The maximum fluorescence emission was obtained at 362 nm, and the fluorescence emission wavelength was 450 nm. The intrinsic mechanism of the CD pH-dependent behavior was revealed through optical properties analysis. The O-related defect and N-related defect states induced by the protolated amino groups on the CD surfaces alternately dominated the fluorescence emission with a change in pH value. The luminescence of different defect states was greatly affected by the excitation wavelength. Based on the fluorescent emission behavior, which could be adjusted with a change in pH, the CDs showed potential application value as an instant inspection.
Dalu Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Yeyan Ni: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2024.136715.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Functional surface engineering of C-dots for fluorescent biosensing and in vivo bioimaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Ding, A. Zhu, Y. Tian, Functional surface engineering of C-dots for fluorescent biosensing and in vivo bioimaging, Acc. Chem. Res. 47(1) (2014) 20-30.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Y.L. Shi, WeiShen, ZhiqiangGao, Carbon quantum dots and their applications, Chem. Soc. Rev. 44, 362–381.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Analytical and bioanalytical applications of carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Silva, H. Gon?Alves, Analytical and bioanalytical applications of carbon dots, TrAC, Trends Anal. Chem. 30(8) (2011) 1327.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis and photoluminescence mechanism of green emitting xylose-derived carbon dots for anti-counterfeit printing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Yang, Z.Q. Zhu, T. Zhang, M.Z. Chen, Y.Z. Cao, W. Zhang, X. Wang, X.Y. Zhou, W.M. Chen, Facile synthesis and photoluminescence mechanism of green emitting xylose-derived carbon dots for anti-counterfeit printing, Carbon 146 (2019) 636-649.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red/green tunable-emission carbon nanodots for smart visual precision pH sensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.L. Zhao, Q.Y. Luo, Q. Ruan, K. Chen, C. Liu, C. Redshaw, Z.W. Jin, Red/green tunable-emission carbon nanodots for smart visual precision pH sensing, Chem. Mater. 33(15) (2021) 6091-6098.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical induced calcium phosphate precipitation: Importance of local pH

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Yang, B. Song, R. Weij De N, M. Saakes, C. Buisman, Electrochemical induced calcium phosphate precipitation: Importance of local pH, Environmental Science & Technology 51(19) (2017) 11156-11164.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in functionalized carbon dots toward the design of efficient materials for sensing and catalysis applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Dhenadhayalan, K.C. Lin, T.A. Saleh, Recent Advances in Functionalized Carbon Dots toward the Design of Efficient Materials for Sensing and Catalysis Applications, Small 16(1) (2020) 24.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: State-of-the-art on the preparation, modification, and application of biomass-derived carbon quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.L. Zhu, D.K. Shen, C.F. Wu, S. Gu, State-of-the-Art on the Preparation, Modification, and Application of Biomass-Derived Carbon Quantum Dots, Ind. Eng. Chem. Res. 59(51) (2020) 22017-22039.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red/orange dual-emissive carbon dots for pH sensing and cell imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.R. Zhang, R.G. Su, J. Zhong, L. Fei, W. Cai, Q.W. Guan, W.J. Li, N. Li, Y.S. Chen, L.L. Cai, Q. Xu, Red/orange dual-emissive carbon dots for pH sensing and cell imaging, Nano Res 12(4) (2019) 815-821.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile and high-yield synthesis of carbon quantum dots from biomass-derived carbons at mild condition

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.S. Jing, Y.S. Zhao, R.C. Sun, L.X. Zhong, X.W. Peng, Facile and high-yield synthesis of carbon quantum dots from biomass-derived carbons at mild condition, Acs Sustainable Chemistry & Engineering 7(8) (2019) 7833-7843.
